title: [Rootless container] sd-bus call: Permission denied: OCI runtime permission denied error
body:
**BUG REPORT**

/kind bug

**Description**

Launching rootless container used to work, but doesn't anymore, and I have no idea what changed since then.  This errors shows up when I try to run a container without root permissions, it works great when launched as root.

**Steps to reproduce the issue:**

1. Running Ubuntu 18.04.4 LTS
It happens on both 
(A) a VM running ubuntu desktop: `5.3.0-53-generic #47~18.04.1-Ubuntu SMP Thu May 7 13:10:50 UTC 2020 x86_64 x86_64 x86_64 GNU/Linux` and 
(B) another baremetal machine running ubuntu server: `4.15.0-101-generic #102-Ubuntu SMP Mon May 11 10:07:26 UTC 2020 x86_64 x86_64 x86_64 GNU/Linux`

2. Changed grub linux arguments to support the unified hierarchy:
```bash
GRUB_CMDLINE_LINUX="systemd.cgroup_unified_hierarchy=1 swapaccount=1"
```

3. Updated grub and rebooted

4. Running this command throws the error:
```console
$ podman run --rm edvgui/alpine-hello-world
Error: sd-bus call: Permission denied: OCI runtime permission denied error
```
This image is built with Docker and works for non-rootless containers.  It does nothing fancy, just execute `/bin/echo "Hello World"` in an Alpine container.

**Describe the results you received:**
<details>
<summary>The full output when executing the command with log-level=debug</summary>

```console

**Describe the results you expected:**

It should simply print `Hello World`
```console
$ podman run --rm edvgui/alpine-hello-world
Hello World
```

**Additional information you deem important (e.g. issue happens only occasionally):**
Systemd version
```console
$ systemd --version
systemd 237
+PAM +AUDIT +SELINUX +IMA +APPARMOR +SMACK +SYSVINIT +UTMP +LIBCRYPTSETUP +GCRYPT +GNUTLS +ACL +XZ +LZ4 +SECCOMP +BLKID +ELFUTILS +KMOD -IDN2 +IDN -PCRE2 default-hierarchy=hybrid
```

Mounted cgroup
```console
$ mount | grep cgroup
cgroup on /sys/fs/cgroup type cgroup2 (rw,nosuid,nodev,noexec,relatime)
```

```
Version:            1.9.2
RemoteAPI Version:  1
Go Version:         go1.10.1
OS/Arch:            linux/amd64
```

comments.body: It looks like for some reason when attempting to communicate with systemd over dbus, you are getting permission denied. @giuseppe WDYT?

We need to use dbus from a user namespace in Podman and the systemd version might be too old.

Can you try the command `$ unshare -r systemd-run --user echo it works` ?

Hi @giuseppe , here is the output of the command:
```console
$ unshare -r systemd-run --user echo it works
Failed to start transient service unit: Access denied
```

Unfortunetly this systemd version (237) is the latest one I can get from the ubuntu repository, does it mean that Podman wouldn't be compatible with ubuntu anymore?  That sounds rough... What changed recently that could have caused this problem?  I am 100% sure that everything was working properly less than a month ago for me.

so the issue is in the too old systemd and that there is no way of creating a cgroup while being in a user namespace.

Were you using cgroup v1 before?

Does it work if you use the cgroupfs driver for podman?

`podman --cgroup-manager cgroupfs ...`

> so the issue is in the too old systemd and that there is no way of creating a cgroup while being in a user namespace.

But there should be a way right?  And it seems that using `crun` standalone works just fine for that mather ([see my first comment](https://github.com/containers/libpod/issues/6368#issuecomment-633060374)).

I was using cgroupv1 before, but as there is no way for an unpriviledged user to manage any control group, which makes the experience with rootless containers quite limited (no resource management), so I moved to cgroupv2.  And I really think it worked at some point.

The cgroupfs doesn't work yet with cgroupv2, so in a **cgroupv2** configuration the result isn't really exciting:
```console
$ podman --cgroup-manager cgroupfs run --rm edvgui/alpine-hello-world
Error: invalid configuration, cannot specify resource limits without cgroups v2 and --cgroup-manager=systemd
```

Going back to **cgroupv1** gives those results:
```console
$ podman run --rm edvgui/alpine-hello-world
Hello World
$ podman --cgroup-manager cgroupfs run --rm edvgui/alpine-hello-world
Hello World
$ podman run --rm --memory 1g edvgui/alpine-hello-world
Error: invalid configuration, cannot specify resource limits without cgroups v2 and --cgroup-manager=systemd
$ podman --cgroup-manager cgroupfs run --rm --memory 1g edvgui/alpine-hello-world
Error: invalid configuration, cannot specify resource limits without cgroups v2 and --cgroup-manager=systemd
```
Can you update to a newer version of Ubuntu?

> But there should be a way right? And it seems that using `crun` standalone works just fine for that mather ([see my first comment](https://github.com/containers/libpod/issues/6368#issuecomment-633060374)).

if you call crun by itself like that, then it is not running in a user namespace.  Rootless podman requires to run in a user namespace so all containers are owned by the same namespace.

> if you call crun by itself like that, then it is not running in a user namespace. Rootless podman requires to run in a user namespace so all containers are owned by the same namespace.

Right, sorry I mixed things up a little bit

> Can you update to a newer version of Ubuntu?

That might mess things up with other dependencies but I can try.  What is the first next version of systemd that would be compatible do you think? (Here are the available ones for the different update I could make https://packages.ubuntu.com/search?keywords=systemd, by default I would go with ubuntu 20.04)

Using ubuntu 20.04, things seem to be fixed, until trying to limit the cpu usage:

```console
$ podman run --rm edvgui/alpine-hello-world
Hello World
$ podman run --rm -m 1g edvgui/alpine-hello-world
Hello World
$ podman run --rm -m 1g --cpus 1 edvgui/alpine-hello-world
Error: writing file `/sys/fs/cgroup/user.slice/user-1000.slice/user@1000.service/cgroup.subtree_control`: No such file or directory: OCI runtime command not found error
```
> Edit: I checked, `/sys/fs/cgroup/user.slice/user-1000.slice/user@1000.service/cgroup.subtree_control` does exist, even from inside podman user namespace:
> ```console
> $ podman unshare bash
> # ls -l /sys/fs/cgroup/user.slice/user-1000.slice/user@1000.service/cgroup.subtree_control 
> -rw-r--r-- 1 root root 0 May 26 12:22 /sys/fs/cgroup/user.slice/user-1000.slice/user@1000.service/cgroup.subtree_control
> ```

Systemd version
```console
$ systemd --version
systemd 245 (245.4-4ubuntu3.1)
+PAM +AUDIT +SELINUX +IMA +APPARMOR +SMACK +SYSVINIT +UTMP +LIBCRYPTSETUP +GCRYPT +GNUTLS +ACL +XZ +LZ4 +SECCOMP +BLKID +ELFUTILS +KMOD +IDN2 -IDN +PCRE2 default-hierarchy=hybrid
```

The issue also occurs when launching non-rootless containers.
Should I open another issue?  I can not tell if it is related to the original one...

> Edit, could this be related to [runc#254](https://github.com/containers/crun/issues/254)?

> Edit 2, I don't have `cpu` in `/sys/fs/cgroup/user.slice/cgroup.controllers`, that might be the issue...
> Edit 3, only `/sys/fs/cgroup/cgroup.controllers` has `cpu` in it, none of the child slice inherited from it, any idea of how this is possible?

> The issue also occurs when launching non-rootless containers.
> Should I open another issue? I can not tell if it is related to the original one...
> 
> >

the cpu controller is not enabled by default in systemd, you need first to enable it.

Does a `echo +cpu > /sys/fs/cgroup/cgroup.subtree_control` fix the issue?

> Does a `echo +cpu > /sys/fs/cgroup/cgroup.subtree_control` fix the issue?

Nope, I get a bad `Invalid Argument` error
```console
$ echo +cpu > /sys/fs/cgroup/cgroup.subtree_control 
-bash: /sys/fs/cgroup/cgroup.subtree_control: Permission denied
$ echo +cpu | sudo tee /sys/fs/cgroup/cgroup.subtree_control 
+cpu
tee: /sys/fs/cgroup/cgroup.subtree_control: Invalid argument
$
$ cat /sys/fs/cgroup/cgroup.controllers 
cpuset cpu io memory pids rdma
$ cat /sys/fs/cgroup/cgroup.subtree_control 
cpuset io memory pids
```

I found this in cgroupv2 documentation:
> WARNING: cgroup2 doesn’t yet support control of realtime processes and the cpu controller can only be enabled when all RT processes are in the root cgroup. Be aware that system management software may already have placed RT processes into nonroot cgroups during the system boot process, and these processes may need to be moved to the root cgroup before the cpu controller can be enabled.

But I don't really understand, what are RT processes?  And how can I check if any of them is not in the root cgroup?
Could you try as root?

It gives the same error
```console
# echo +cpu > /sys/fs/cgroup/cgroup.subtree_control
bash: echo: write error: Invalid argument
```

> ```
> ```
> 
> ```
> 
> 
> I found this in cgroupv2 documentation:
> 
> WARNING: cgroup2 doesn’t yet support control of realtime processes and the cpu controller can only be enabled when all RT processes are in the root cgroup. Be aware that system management software may already have placed RT processes into nonroot cgroups during the system boot process, and these processes may need to be moved to the root cgroup before the cpu controller can be enabled.
> 
> But I don't really understand, what are RT processes?  And how can I check if any of them is not in the root cgroup?
> ```

yes that could be the reason why you cannot enable the cpu controller.  To find out what RT (real time) processes you have, you can use the command: `ps ax -L -o 'pid tid cls rtprio comm' |grep RR
`

@giuseppe THANK YOU! It finally worked!
The command you showed me revealed that one RT process was running: `multipathd` and killing it (I don't know how to simply move it the the root cgroup) solved the problem right away.

This has to be done on each reboot though and I don't really understand what `multipathd` is about, in which control group it goes on boot or even if killing it might do any harm...  But I can finally go on with rootless podman, after 5 days, and it feels good.

> Edit: `multipathd` is actually a service managed by systemd, restarting it instead of wildly killing it works as good and is probably better (`systemctl restart multipathd.service`)
What's our status here - is this solved, or are there lingering questions?

The original issue isn't solve, but going with a newer version of Ubuntu allowed me to get things working anyway.  I will close it and I guess that if someone else faces the same problem I had, he can still reopen it.

Just for documentation:
It is still a valid issue on Debian 10 with podman 2.0.4, cgroupv2 and systemd 241. 

like @giuseppe already said:

> so the issue is in the too old systemd and that there is no way of creating a cgroup while being in a user namespace.

Running with cgroupfs like `podman --cgroup-manager=cgroupfs run --rm -it alpine` works.

Seems like my report is related to this one as well:
https://github.com/containers/podman/issues/7650


How can we fix this? What's the disadvantage of using ```cgroup-manager=cgroupfs```? Shouldn't than cgroup-manager=cgroupfs be the default under debian 10? Why did it work fine with podman 1.9.4?

If the debian packager wants to change the default, they can simply ship a containers.conf in /usr/share/containers/containers.conf with this settings. 
Is this true for all Debian or just the stable branch?
@rhatdan sure. I just wanted to know why this wasn't a problem with 1.9.4 and what's the disadvantage of cgroup-manager=cgroupfs

The issue is with cgroupv2, I believe.  cgroupfs can not work in rootless mode with cgroupv2 since you need to communicate with systemd to setup the cgroups and other controllers.  @giuseppe Could give more definitive answers.  Since going forward cgroupv2 will become the default, the master branch defaults to systemd.

mhm @rhatdan i'm only using cgroupv2 never used v1.
```
# mount | grep cgroup
cgroup2 on /sys/fs/cgroup type cgroup2 (rw,nosuid,nodev,noexec,relatime
```

podman 1.9.4 worked fine using defaults

podman 2.0.6 stopped working using defaults but works with: ```--cgroup-manager=cgroupfs```
